Step 3: Put your top foot in front of your bottom foot.
Step 4: Find your neutral spine and engage your core to lock it in place. 
Step 5: Slowly lift your hips off the ground as you bring them forward to form a straight line from foot to shoulders, like a plank. You do this to avoid side bending in the spine. This motion is the same as for the bridge except it is done on your side; in other words, you are are bringing your pelvis forward as you come up, going from bent hips to straightened hips so that you don’t bend your spine sideways. 

Step 6: If you can, raise your upper arm up toward the ceiling with the palm facing forward, and pull that shoulder blade back toward the spine and buttocks as well.
Step 7: Hold for five to ten seconds, with ten seconds being the goal.
Step 8: Slowly “sit back,” pushing your hips back behind your feet and shoulders as you return to the floor in the starting position. Again, think about the movement of the bridge exercise, only done on your side.
Step 9: Do two to five reps on each side, with five being the goal.
TROUBLESHOOTING

• Shoulder pain. If your shoulder hurts, make sure you pay attention to Step 1. Is your elbow directly under your shoulder? Did you pull your shoulder blade in toward the spine and down toward your back pocket before lifting up? If you feel you did these motions correctly and your shoulder still hurts, try bracing your shoulder with your upper hand as in the following picture.
If it still hurts, move on to one of the following regressions on the next page, and perform these until you build st …[truncated]

I'm glad you're interested in exploring textures! Since you mentioned attending an art fair and seeing amazing works by local artists, I assume you're looking for techniques that can add an extra layer of depth and interest to your paintings.

Considering your interest in seascape paintings, I think techniques like salt sprinkling, wash and scrape, and drybrushing could be great options. These methods can help you achieve a sense of movement and energy in your waves, as well as add texture to your painting.

If you're looking for more experimental approaches, you could try incorporating collage elements or using pumice gel to create a rough, rocky surface. These techniques can add a unique, three-dimensional quality to your painting.

Remember, the key is to experiment and find the techniques that work best for you and your artistic vision. Don't be afraid to try new things and make mistakes – they can often lead to exciting, unexpected results!

I think I'll go with the traditional Indian colors of red and gold for the title, and use a softer coral and pink palette for the rest of the text. I like the idea of using a cream or beige background to let the fonts and colors stand out. Do you think it's a good idea to add some Indian-inspired patterns or motifs to the design, such as henna patterns or Indian florals?

I think I'll add some subtle henna patterns as accents to the design, maybe in the corners of the pages or as separators between sections. I also like the idea of incorporating Indian florals, maybe using delicate illustrations of marigolds or jasmine flowers to add a touch of elegance to the design.
